headline: First-ever pipeline capture: Treasury cancelled all 31 Booz Allen contracts ($21M total obligations) on January 26, 2026 over Littlejohn IRS leak (406K taxpayers); April 2026 Maryland federal court refused to dismiss class action — sustained insider-threat governance failure
methodology_notes: First pipeline capture for this entity — published 48.4 is the F500 Gen-2 3×7+2.5×1 uniform profile (rank-bracket 100-150 cluster) and is not evidence-grounded. The unique aspect of this entity is that the Treasury cancellation is a first-of-its-kind sovereign-counterparty action: the principal U.S. Treasury, after a multi-year insider-threat investigation by IRS-CI and DOJ, formally severed all contracts — not a settlement, not a fine, but an outright revocation of trust. This is a Tier-1 governance signal that goes directly to INT (consistency between stated values and verified practices) and ACC (institutional accountability). INT lowered to 12.5 — Booz Allen's public response ('Littlejohn's conduct was on government systems, not Booz Allen systems') is in tension with Treasury Secretary Bessent's stated rationale (vetting and ongoing-monitoring failure). ACC at 25.0 reflects post-hoc accountability via revenue loss and stock decline (>10%) but no proactive disclosure of the supervisory gap. April 2026 Maryland federal court ruling (Judge declined to dismiss IRS+Booz Allen from class action) extends the accountability exposure into the assessment window. Senator Rick Scott's separate suit (March 24, 2026) is supplementary evidence. Cluster-artifact correction is secondary to the substantive INT/ACC governance event — this is NOT primarily a ceiling correction; it is a first baseline against documented institutional failure. Band change Functional → Developing.

evidence.claim: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent announced cancellation of all 31 Treasury contracts with Booz Allen Hamilton on January 26, 2026, citing the firm's 'past insider threat incident' as rationale; contracts totaled $4.8M annual spending and $21M total obligations
Booz Allen Hamilton stock fell more than 10% on the Treasury announcement (January 26, 2026)

Charles Edward Littlejohn (Booz Allen IRS contractor 2018-2020) stole and leaked tax records of approximately 406,000 taxpayers including Trump, Bezos, and Musk; sentenced January 2024 to 5 years federal prison after pleading guilty to one count of unauthorized disclosure

April 2026 Maryland federal judge ruling: court refused to dismiss class action against Booz Allen and IRS over the Littlejohn data breach — case survives motion to dismiss, plaintiffs may continue pursuing damages
March 24, 2026: Senator Rick Scott (R-FL) filed separate civil suit against Booz Allen over the Littlejohn leak — extends accountability pressure into Q1-Q2 2026

Booz Allen public statement: 'When Littlejohn's criminal conduct occurred over 5 years ago, it was on government systems, not Booz Allen systems...Booz Allen stores no taxpayer data on its systems' — characterization in tension with Treasury rationale and federal court findings
Treasury Secretary explanation framing the action as IRS contractor vetting failure (Booz Allen's preventive controls) rather than as government-systems issue

key_evidence: Treasury cancelled all 31 contracts ($21M) January 26, 2026 — sovereign-counterparty trust revocation, not settlement
April 2026 Maryland federal court refused to dismiss IRS+Booz Allen class action over Littlejohn breach (406K taxpayers affected)
Senator Rick Scott separate civil suit filed March 24, 2026
Booz Allen public stance ('government systems, not Booz Allen systems') in tension with Treasury rationale and court findings — INT 12.5 reflects this gap
Stock price fell >10% on Treasury announcement; ACC 25.0 reflects post-hoc market accountability without proactive disclosure
First pipeline capture; published 48.4 is uniform Gen-2 3×7+2.5×1 cluster artifact, not evidence-grounded
